content.text: Assessing technical feasibility for agentic AI integration is the structured process of determining whether an organization's existing APIs, infrastructure, models, and operational tooling can support an autonomous multi-step AI system before committing significant engineering resources. Unlike feasibility checks for conventional software, agentic systems introduce unique constraints: model reasoning quality, tool-call latency chains, orchestrator reliability, and unpredictable execution paths all compound in ways that make shallow assessments misleading.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI projects fail disproportionately at integration boundaries. A model that performs well in isolation can become unreliable once chained through five sequential tool calls, each adding latency, failure probability, and surface area for hallucinated outputs. Without a rigorous feasibility check at discovery time, teams discover these barriers only after months of development work.

Enterprise delivery programmes treat technical feasibility as a funding gate. Failing to surface integration blockers before sprint planning leads to budget overruns, late architectural pivots, and reputational damage to the AI programme. A structured assessment converts vague enthusiasm into documented evidence that either justifies proceeding or triggers a targeted remediation plan.
Benefits
Surfaces integration blockers (missing APIs, auth gaps, latency violations) weeks before they become delivery risks.
Produces a weighted score that is legible to non-technical stakeholders and directly tied to funding decisions.
Forces model capability testing against actual task descriptions rather than benchmark proxies.
Creates an audit-ready artefact that documents architectural assumptions for security and compliance review.
Reduces PoC scope by identifying which integration points need stub or mock implementations before real APIs are available.
Trade-offs

A thorough feasibility assessment takes one to three weeks of an architect's time. For small-scale experiments, this overhead outweighs the risk it mitigates. The assessment also risks producing false confidence if evaluators only test the happy path: an API may respond successfully in isolation but fail under concurrent agentic load or when called from a restricted network segment.

Model capability assessment is inherently probabilistic. Benchmark scores on reasoning tasks do not guarantee the same quality on domain-specific workflows. Evaluators must run representative task samples, not rely on published numbers. This adds cost but is non-negotiable for regulated industries where model failure has legal consequences.
How It Works

Technical feasibility assessment for agentic AI is organised around five pillars: API availability, model capability, latency profile, infrastructure readiness, and security posture. Each pillar is scored independently on a 0–100 scale, then combined using a weighted average. The overall score determines a go/no-go/conditional recommendation.

API availability covers whether the external and internal services the agent must call expose programmable interfaces with adequate documentation, authentication support, and rate limits that align with expected call volumes. Missing APIs are the most common blocker in enterprise settings, where legacy systems often expose data only through batch exports or vendor-specific protocols.

Model capability assessment evaluates whether available model variants — proprietary or open-weight — can reliably complete the specific reasoning tasks the agent requires: tool selection, multi-hop planning, structured output generation, and domain-specific understanding. Evaluators run a representative sample of 50–100 task instances through candidate models, scoring accuracy, instruction-following consistency, and JSON schema adherence.

Latency profiling measures end-to-end round-trip times for each tool call in a representative workflow, sums them across a realistic agent trajectory (often 4–12 sequential LLM + tool steps), and compares the total against the user-facing SLA. Agentic tasks frequently violate latency budgets that single-shot LLM calls would satisfy.

Infrastructure readiness checks whether the orchestration layer (LangGraph, Temporal, custom worker pools), observability stack (distributed tracing, structured logging), and secret management systems are mature enough to support a production agentic deployment. The presence of containerised workloads and CI/CD pipelines is a strong positive signal; hand-managed VMs with manual deployments are a risk factor.
Architecture

The assessment flows from five independent pillars through a scoring engine to a go/no-go gate, with passing projects moving to PoC resource allocation and failing projects entering a gap-remediation track.

attrs.alt: Technical Feasibility Assessment pillars feeding a weighted scoring engine and go/no-go decision gate

content.text: When to Use It

Run a full technical feasibility assessment when a use case has cleared an ideation filter and is being considered for PoC funding. Skip it only for trivial single-tool integrations where the integration surface is already well-understood from prior work. Use an abbreviated version (latency and API pillars only) for incremental additions to an established agentic platform.

Do not use it as a rubber-stamp. If evaluators feel pressure to score a pillar higher than evidence supports to secure executive backing, the assessment has lost its value as a risk gate. Scoring should be performed by engineers who will actually build the system, with review by an independent architect.
How to Implement
The following Python class implements the five-pillar scoring model. Each pillar score is provided by an assessor (0–100); the class computes the weighted aggregate and returns a recommendation.
Examples
Example 1: Running an Assessment

assessment = FeasibilityAssessment(
    project_name="Procurement Approval Agent",
    pillar_scores=[
        PillarScore(
            name="api_availability",
            score=85,
            evidence="ERP exposes REST endpoints; SAP connector library available",
        ),
        PillarScore(
            name="model_capability",
            score=78,
            evidence="GPT-4o scored 82% on 50 sample approval tasks",
        ),
        PillarScore(
            name="latency_profile",
            score=55,
            evidence="8-step trajectory totals ~14s; SLA is 10s",
            blockers=[
                "ERP search endpoint averages 3.2s; requires caching layer",
                "Sequential tool calls need partial parallelisation",
            ],
        ),
        PillarScore(
            name="infra_readiness",
            score=80,
            evidence="Kubernetes cluster with LangGraph worker pool already running",
        ),
        PillarScore(
            name="security_posture",
            score=70,
            evidence="Vault-managed secrets; PII fields excluded from tool responses",
        ),
    ],
)

print(assessment.report())
# Weighted Score: 73.8/100
# Recommendation: CONDITIONAL
Example 2: API Probe Script
Before scoring the API availability pillar, evaluators should probe each required endpoint programmatically rather than relying on documentation claims.

import time
import httpx
from typing import TypedDict


class ProbeResult(TypedDict):
    endpoint: str
    reachable: bool
    auth_ok: bool
    p50_ms: float
    status_code: int
    notes: str


def probe_endpoint(
    url: str,
    headers: dict[str, str],
    samples: int = 5,
) -> ProbeResult:
    latencies = []
    last_status = 0
    auth_ok = False
    reachable = False

    for _ in range(samples):
        try:
            t0 = time.perf_counter()
            resp = httpx.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=10)
            latencies.append((time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000)
            last_status = resp.status_code
            reachable = True
            auth_ok = resp.status_code not in (401, 403)
        except httpx.RequestError as exc:
            return ProbeResult(
                endpoint=url, reachable=False, auth_ok=False,
                p50_ms=0.0, status_code=0, notes=str(exc)
            )

    latencies.sort()
    p50 = latencies[len(latencies) // 2]
    return ProbeResult(
        endpoint=url,
        reachable=reachable,
        auth_ok=auth_ok,
        p50_ms=round(p50, 1),
        status_code=last_status,
        notes="",
    )


# Usage
result = probe_endpoint(
    "https://erp.internal/api/v2/purchase-orders",
    headers={"Authorization": "Bearer <token>"},
)
print(result)
Real-World Example

A financial services firm evaluated an agentic loan-processing assistant. The API availability pillar scored 90 because the core loan management system already had a mature REST API. However, the latency pillar scored 48: the credit-bureau enrichment call (required on every application) returned in 6–9 seconds, and the target UX required sub-five-second responses. The model capability pillar scored 72 on a 60-application sample.

The aggregate score of 71 yielded a conditional recommendation. The PoC brief was modified to include a pre-fetch credit enrichment cache (reducing the bottleneck by ~70%) and a streaming response UI that displays partial results as each tool step completes. Both mitigations were confirmed feasible within the PoC timeline before funding was approved. Without the latency probe, engineers would have discovered the constraint only after building the full orchestration layer.
Common Mistakes

Testing model capability on benchmark datasets instead of domain-representative task samples. Public benchmarks measure average capability; your use case has specific format, vocabulary, and failure modes.
Measuring single-call latency rather than end-to-end agent trajectory latency. An agent that makes eight tool calls, each at 500 ms, takes four seconds even before adding model inference time.
Ignoring rate limits. APIs that respond fine at one call per second may throttle at the concurrent volumes an agent generates during batch tasks.

Conflating infrastructure readiness for a web service with readiness for a stateful long-running agent. Agentic systems need durable execution, checkpoint storage, and idempotent retry logic that most web infrastructure does not provide by default.

Scoring security based on documentation rather than tested configurations. Verify that the agent's service account can only access the exact resources it needs, not that the access control policy claims it can.
Best Practices

Assign pillar ownership to subject-matter engineers: the API pillar to the integration team, the model pillar to an ML engineer, the security pillar to the security architect. Cross-functional ownership prevents optimistic scoring.

Document evidence for every score in the assessment artefact. A score of 80 with no evidence is meaningless; a score of 80 citing 'ERP v2 REST API tested at 200 req/min with OAuth 2.0 client-credentials flow' is actionable.
Re-run the assessment after significant scope changes. An agent that initially required four APIs and now requires ten has a materially different API availability score.
For conditional scores (60–79), produce a gap remediation plan with named owners and estimated effort before the gate review. Conditional should never mean 'we'll figure it out during the PoC.'
Version the assessment document and store it alongside the PoC repository. Post-project retrospectives are far more informative when the original feasibility assumptions are still accessible.
Performance and Scalability

The latency pillar must be evaluated under projected load, not just at idle. Agents running in customer-facing applications may need to support hundreds of concurrent sessions. An API that handles one call per second trivially may fail at 50 concurrent calls due to connection pooling limits, database lock contention in the backing service, or upstream rate limiting by the API provider.

Use load-testing tools (Locust, k6) to simulate realistic concurrency on each integration endpoint during feasibility. If the target API provider exposes sandbox environments, run concurrency tests there. For production-only APIs, negotiate a brief load-test window with the API owner as part of the assessment process.

LLM inference latency scales non-linearly with context window usage. A model that responds in 800 ms for a 2,000-token context may take 3,500 ms for an 8,000-token context when the agent's working memory accumulates over a long trajectory. Measure latency at expected mid-trajectory context sizes, not just at the initial prompt size.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Several tools assist with specific pillars of the assessment:
API availability: Postman Collections or Bruno for endpoint inventory; OpenAPI validator for schema completeness; httpx or requests for probe scripting.
Model capability: RAGAS evaluation framework for RAG-augmented tasks; custom eval harnesses using the provider's batch inference API; PromptFoo for multi-model comparison.
Latency: k6 for load-profile simulation; OpenTelemetry SDK for distributed trace collection; Jaeger or Grafana Tempo for visualising multi-hop latency.
Infrastructure readiness: Checkov for IaC policy scanning; Pulumi or Terraform plan outputs for drift detection; SLO dashboards to verify existing reliability baselines.
Security posture: Cloud provider IAM access analyser for least-privilege verification; Vault audit logs for secrets access patterns; OWASP API Security Top 10 checklist for API surface review.
Comparison with Alternatives
Three common approaches to technical feasibility exist: informal gut-check, full architectural spike, and the structured scoring model described here.
Approach
Time Required

Stakeholder Legibility
Best For

Security and Reliability

The security pillar of the assessment should verify that the agent's service identity uses the minimum permissions necessary (principle of least privilege). Agents that browse enterprise APIs frequently accumulate ambient authority — a service account with broad read access granted during initial development and never tightened. The feasibility check is the right moment to lock down these permissions before they become architectural debt.

Reliability assessment should map each integration point to its historical availability SLA and calculate the compound availability of the full agent trajectory. If an agent depends on eight independent services each at 99.5% availability, the agent's own availability ceiling is 0.995^8 ≈ 96.1%, which may be insufficient for business-critical tasks. Feasibility must flag when the integration topology structurally prevents reliability targets from being met.
Mental Model

Think of the feasibility assessment as a pre-flight checklist for an aircraft. Each pillar is a system check — engine, fuel, avionics, weather, crew. All checks must pass a minimum threshold before departure; a single critical failure grounds the flight regardless of how good everything else looks. The weighted aggregate score represents overall flight readiness, but a single pillar scoring below 40 is an automatic no-go regardless of the aggregate, just as a failed engine check grounds the aircraft even if fuel and avionics are perfect.
Key Takeaways

Technical feasibility for agentic AI spans five pillars: API availability, model capability, latency profile, infrastructure readiness, and security posture — each scored 0–100 with documented evidence.
Latency must be measured end-to-end across a full agent trajectory, not per single tool call, because sequential call chains multiply individual delays.
Model capability must be evaluated on domain-representative task samples, not published benchmarks, since benchmark performance does not transfer directly to specific business workflows.
A conditional recommendation (score 60–79) must be paired with a concrete gap remediation plan with named owners before PoC funding is approved.

Compound availability — the product of individual integration point SLAs — often creates a structural reliability ceiling that no amount of engineering can overcome without reducing integration dependencies.
The assessment artefact should be versioned alongside the codebase to enable post-project retrospectives comparing original assumptions against measured outcomes.
